    Coma Cluster (Abell 1656)

    Coma Cluster

    Type:Galaxy cluster
    Redshift:z = 0.023
    Distance:~100 Mpc
    BCGs:NGC 4874, NGC 4889
    M200:~0.5–2.9 × 1015 M☉ (method-dependent)
    First mass estimate:Zwicky (1933) [1]
    Related: NFW Profile
    Cluster Substructures
    Ram-Pressure Stripping

    The Coma Cluster (Abell 1656) is one of the nearest rich galaxy clusters and has been central to astrophysics since Zwicky's (1933) pioneering inference of dark matter from its galaxy velocity dispersion [1]. Located at z = 0.023, it contains thousands of galaxies dominated by massive red quiescent early-type galaxies alongside a smaller population of blue star-forming late-types [2][3].

    Mass Estimates

    Coma is a benchmark for comparing cluster mass estimation methods. Estimates span a wide range depending on technique:

    • Kinematic (Jeans): M200 = 1.2 × 1015 M☉ from elliptical galaxies [4]; 1.08 × 1015 M☉ from DESI DR1 [5]
    • Weak lensing: 5.1 × 1014 M☉ [6]; 7.4 × 1014 M☉ from CNN methods [7]
    • X-ray + SZ: 8.5 × 1014 M☉ [8]
    • Bayesian deep learning: 1.8 × 1015 M☉ [9]
    • Caustics/virial: 1.1–2.9 × 1015 M☉ [10]
    ⚖️ Ongoing tension: Kinematic and caustic methods consistently yield higher masses (~1015 M☉) than weak lensing (~5–7 × 1014 M☉). Possible explanations include projection effects, substructure contamination in kinematic samples, and mass-sheet degeneracy in lensing [5][6][7].

    Substructure and Merger History

    Despite being long considered a prototype relaxed cluster [11], Coma hosts significant substructure [12][13]:

    • NGC 4839 group: Identified in X-ray maps and galaxy distributions [14][15][16], currently on secondary infall after first apocentre [17][18][19]
    • NGC 4889/NGC 4874 merger: Evidence for a recent or ongoing merger between the two dominant galaxies' parent groups [20]
    • Faint substructures: Many additional groups recently identified [12][13]

    These complex interactions may drive the post-starburst galaxies observed in the Coma periphery [21][22].

    Galaxy Populations and Kinematics

    DESI DR1 spectroscopy of 1651 member galaxies reveals population-dependent kinematics [5]. Red sequence galaxies show near-Gaussian velocity distributions (virialised), while blue cloud galaxies are strongly non-Gaussian (infalling on radial orbits) [5]. This supports ram-pressure stripping as the dominant quenching mechanism [5][23][24].

    See Also

    • Pedratti et al. (2026) — DESI kinematic analysis
    • Orbital Anisotropy
    • Mass Estimation Methods

    References
    1. Zwicky, F. (1933). "Die Rotverschiebung von extragalaktischen Nebeln." Helv. Phys. Acta, 6, 110.
    2. Dressler, A., et al. (1997). "Galaxy populations in clusters." ApJ.
    3. Boselli, A., Gavazzi, G. (2006). "Environmental effects on galaxies." PASP.
    4. Łokas, E. L., Mamon, G. A. (2003). "Dark matter in the Coma cluster." MNRAS, 343, 401.
    5. Pedratti, S., et al. (2026). "Coma Cluster kinematics with DESI. I." arXiv:2603.16706
    6. Gavazzi, R., et al. (2009). "Weak lensing mass of Coma." A&A.
    7. Cha, S., et al. (2025). "Deep-learning weak lensing." ApJ. DOI: 10.3847/1538-4357/adb1b7
    8. Mirakhor, M. S., Walker, S. A. (2020). "XMM + Planck SZ mass." MNRAS. DOI: 10.1093/mnras/staa2203
    9. Ho, M., et al. (2022). "Bayesian deep learning cluster mass." Nat. Astron.
    10. Benisty, D., et al. (2025). "Caustics and virial mass of Coma." arXiv:2504.04135
    11. Biviano, A. (1998). "Subclustering in Coma." Proc. IAU.
    12. Kang, J., et al. (2025). "Substructures in Coma." ApJ.
    13. Costa, A. P., et al. (2025). "Faint substructures in Coma." ApJ.
    14. Baier, F. W., et al. (1990). "Projected galaxy distribution in Coma." Astron. Nachr.
    15. White, S. D. M., et al. (1993). "X-ray maps of Coma." Nature.
    16. Neumann, D. M., et al. (2001). "NGC 4839 group in X-rays." A&A.
    17. Biviano, A., et al. (1996). "NGC 4839 group dynamics." A&A.
    18. Lyskova, N., et al. (2019). "NGC 4839 secondary infall." MNRAS.
    19. Churazov, E., et al. (2021). "NGC 4839 infall dynamics." A&A.
    20. Adami, C., et al. (2005). "Coma merger history." A&A.
    21. Caldwell, N., et al. (1993). "Post-starburst galaxies in Coma." AJ.
    22. Gavazzi, G., et al. (2010). "Post-starburst galaxies in Coma periphery." A&A. DOI: 10.1051/0004-6361/201014153
    23. Gunn, J. E., Gott, J. R. (1972). "On the Infall of Matter into Clusters." ApJ, 176, 1.
    24. Boselli, A., Fossati, M., Sun, M. (2022). "Ram-pressure stripping review." A&ARv.
